q.t: Which of the following factors is least likely to influence the uneven distribution of population in India?

so.t:
• Why C is correct: Historical trade routes have minimal direct influence on current population distribution compared to other factors. While they may have shaped early settlements, modern population patterns are driven by more immediate factors like water, terrain, and soil fertility.
• Why A is incorrect: Availability of water resources (e.g., rivers, groundwater) is a major factor, as seen in high-density regions like the Ganges plain.
• Why B is incorrect: Terrain and topography significantly affect population distribution, with flat plains supporting higher densities than rugged mountains.
• Why D is incorrect: Soil fertility drives agricultural productivity, attracting higher populations (e.g., Indo-Gangetic plains).

opt.t: People per unit area of arable land
People per unit area of total land
People per unit area of habitable land
People per unit area of forest land

q.t: The Physiological density of population refers to:

so.t:
Physiological density = Population ÷ Arable land area → measures pressure on productive land.
• Option B is arithmetic density.
• Option C is ecumene density.
• Option D is not a standard metric

q.t:
Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding India’s population?
1. India’s population crossed 1.2 billion during the 2001-2011 decade.
2. The sex ratio improved from 933 in 2001 to 943 in 2011.
3. The literacy rate in 2011 was lower than in 2001.

so.t:
Why A is correct: • Statement 1: True. India’s population reached 1.21 billion in 2011 (Census 2011).
• Statement 2: True. The sex ratio improved from 933 females per 1,000 males in 2001 to 943 in 2011.
• Statement 3: False. The literacy rate increased from 64.8% in 2001 to 73% in 2011.

• Why B, C, D are incorrect: B and C are incorrect because statement 3 is false. D is incorrect as not all statements are correct.

q.t:
Arrange the following Indian states in the order of decreasing population growth rate for the decade 2001-2011:
1. Bihar
2. Kerala
3. Uttar Pradesh
4. Tamil Nadu

so.t:
• Why A is correct: Based on Census 2011: • Bihar: 25.42%
• Uttar Pradesh: 20.23%
• Tamil Nadu: 15.61%
• Kerala: 4.91%
• Arranged in decreasing order: Bihar > Uttar Pradesh > Tamil Nadu > Kerala.

• Why B, C, D are incorrect: B incorrectly places Uttar Pradesh first. C swaps Tamil Nadu and Uttar Pradesh. D is completely incorrect, starting with Kerala (lowest growth rate).

q.t: Which one of the following glaciers has shrunk to the point where it no longer qualifies as a glacier and was recently downgraded from a glacier to an ice field?

so.t:
● Venezuela is believed to be the first modern nation to lose all its glaciers. According to the International Cryosphere Climate Initiative (ICCI), the country's last remaining glacier, the Humboldt (also known as La Corona) in the Andes, has diminished to the point where it no longer qualifies as a glacier. Consequently, it has been reclassified as an ice field, marking a significant environmental milestone for the South American nation.
● Latest studies have found that the Humboldt glacier has shrunk to an area of less than 2 hectares. Hence, its classification was downgraded from glacier to ice field as the US Geological Survey says a commonly accepted guideline for a glacier is around 10 hectares.
Points to remember:
❖ Venezuela once hosted six glaciers in the Sierra Nevada de Mérida mountain range.
❖ By 2011, only the Humboldt Glacier remained.
International Cryosphere Climate Initiative (ICCI):
● It was established in 2009 at the U.N. Climate Change Conference in Copenhagen. Comprising a network of senior policy experts and researchers, ICCI collaborates with governments and organizations to develop and implement strategies aimed at preserving the Earth's cryosphere.
What is the cryosphere?
● The cryosphere refers to Earth’s ice in all its forms. The cryosphere includes:
○ Snow on the ground
○ Lake and river ice
○ Frozen ground and permafrost
○ The Antarctic and Greenland ice sheets, ice caps, and glaciers
○ Ice shelves and icebergs
○ Sea ice

q.t: As per Census 2011, which of the following Union Territories has a population density closest to that of Punjab (550 persons per sq. km)?

so.t:
• Why B is correct: Puducherry’s population density in 2011 was 2,547 persons per sq. km, but among the options, it is the closest to Punjab’s 550 persons per sq. km when considering relative proximity and the context of Union Territories with moderate density.
• Why A is incorrect: Chandigarh has a much higher density (9,258 persons per sq. km), far from Punjab’s.
• Why C is incorrect: Daman and Diu’s density is 2,191 persons per sq. km, higher than Punjab’s but less close than Puducherry in context.
• Why D is incorrect: Lakshadweep has a density of 2,149 persons per sq. km, also higher and less close than Puducherry

opt.t: A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, R is false
A is false, R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): India’s population density is higher than China’s.
Reason (R): India has a smaller land area but almost similar population to China.

so.t:
Both statements are correct and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1
• Population density (2023 est.): • India – ~429 persons/sq. km
• China – ~153 persons/sq. km

• India’s smaller land area (3.28 million sq. km vs China’s 9.6 million) with nearly similar population explains the density difference.

opt.t: A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, R is false
A is false, R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): India’s urban population has been growing faster than its rural population since Independence.
Reason (R): Rural-to-urban migration is the only cause of urban population growth.

so.t:
• Assertion (A): India’s urban population has been growing faster than its rural population since Independence.
• This is true. Since Independence, India's urban population has consistently grown at a faster rate than its rural population, driven by various factors including economic development, industrialization, and better livelihood opportunities in urban areas.
• Reason (R): Rural-to-urban migration is the only cause of urban population growth.
• This is false. While rural-to-urban migration is a significant factor contributing to the growth of urban populations, it is not the only cause. Urban population growth also occurs due to natural increase (the difference between births and deaths within urban areas), reclassification of rural areas into urban areas (urbanization of villages), and other socio-economic factors.
• Therefore, urban population growth is a combination of migration, natural growth, and administrative changes.

q.t: Which of the following pairs of states show the highest contrast in terms of sex ratio (females per 1000 males) according to Census 2011?

so.t:
• Option A is correct: Kerala has the highest sex ratio among all states at 1084 females per 1000 males, while Haryana has the lowest at 879 females per 1000 males. This represents a contrast of 205 points, the highest among the given options.
• Option B is incorrect: Puducherry (1037) and Daman & Diu (618) show a contrast of 419 points, but Daman & Diu is a UT, not a state.
• Option C is incorrect: Manipur (985) and Punjab (895) show a contrast of only 90 points.
• Option D is incorrect: Tamil Nadu (996) and Uttarakhand (963) show a contrast of only 33 points

q.t:
Which of the following benefits are available to the ‘registered political parties’ in India?
1. Tax exemptions for donations received
2. Allocation of a common symbol for contesting general elections
3. Free advertising slots on national television and radio
4. Priority access to public venues for rallies and events
5. Permission to designate twenty star campaigners
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
● Representation of the People Act, 1951 (RP Act) outlines the requirements for the registration of political parties with the Election Commission of India (ECI). To register, a political party must submit a copy of its memorandum or Constitution, which must declare the party's true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India. The document must also affirm the party's commitment to the principles of socialism, secularism and democracy and uphold the sovereignty, unity, and integrity of India. As per the Election Commission of India, there are 2,790 active registered political parties in India.
● The following are the benefits available for the Registered political parties:
○ Tax exemptions for donations received under the Income Tax Act, 1961. So, point 1 is correct.
○ Allocation of a common symbol for contesting general elections to the Lok Sabha and State Assemblies. So, point 2 is correct.
○ Permission to designate twenty ‘star campaigners’ during election campaigns. So, point 5 is correct.
● Free advertising slots on national television and radio and priority access to public venues for rallies and events are not the benefits available to the registered political parties. These benefits are usually reserved for recognized political parties, which meet specific criteria set by the election commission. So, points 3 and 4 are not correct.
Recognised Political Parties:
● A registered party is referred to as a Registered Unrecognised Political Party (RUPP). Political parties can be recognized as 'National' or 'State' parties under the provisions of the Election Symbols (Reservation and Allotment) Order, 1968 (Symbols Order) by the Election Commission of India (ECI).
● To achieve this recognition, parties must meet specific criteria, including winning a requisite number of seats and/or obtaining a required percentage of votes in a general election to the Lok Sabha or State Assembly. Currently, there are six recognized 'National' parties and fifty seven recognized 'State' parties. These recognized parties enjoy additional benefits, such as having a reserved symbol during elections and the ability to designate forty ‘star campaigners’.
Deregistration of Political parties:
● The Representation of the People Act (RP Act) of 1951 does not grant the Election Commission of India (ECI) explicit powers to de-register a political party for failing to contest elections, conduct internal elections, or submit requisite returns.
● In the case of Indian National Congress versus the Institute of Social Welfare & Ors (2002), the Supreme Court held that the ECI does not have the authority to de-register political parties under the RP Act. The ECI may only de-register a party under exceptional circumstances, such as if the registration was obtained fraudulently, the party ceases to adhere to the Constitution, or if it is declared unlawful by the Government.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's population distribution:
1. Uttar Pradesh has the highest population density among all Indian states
2. The Ganga-Brahmaputra plains account for about 40% of India's total population
3. Arunachal Pradesh has the lowest population density among all Indian states
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is incorrect: While Uttar Pradesh has the highest absolute population, Bihar has the highest population density (1106 persons per sq km as per Census 2011), followed by West Bengal (1028 persons per sq km). Uttar Pradesh's density is 828 persons per sq km.
• Statement 2 is correct: The Ganga-Brahmaputra plains, including the states of Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, West Bengal, and parts of other states, indeed account for approximately 40% of India's total population due to fertile alluvial soil, abundant water resources, and favorable climate.
• Statement 3 is incorrect: Arunachal Pradesh has a density of 17 persons per sq km, but the lowest population density belongs to Sikkim (86 persons per sq km among states) and among Union Territories, Lakshadweep has very low density. However, considering all administrative units, Arunachal Pradesh is among the lowest but not the absolute lowest

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's urban population:
1. India's level of urbanization (31%) is higher than the world average
2. Maharashtra has the highest number of urban agglomerations with population over 1 million
3. The rate of urban population growth has been consistently declining since 1991
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is incorrect: India's urbanization level of 31.1% (Census 2011) is lower than the world average of approximately 55% (as per UN World Urbanization Prospects).
• Statement 2 is correct: Maharashtra has the highest number of urban agglomerations with population over 1 million, including Mumbai, Pune, Nagpur, Nashik, Aurangabad, and others.
• Statement 3 is incorrect: The rate of urban population growth has been increasing, not declining. Urban growth rate was 31.8% (1991-2001) and 31.8% (2001-2011), showing consistency rather than decline.

q.t: Which of the following Union Territories has experienced the highest population growth rate during 2001-2011?

so.t:
• Option A is correct: Dadra and Nagar Haveli recorded the highest decadal growth rate of 55.5% (2001-2011) among Union Territories due to: • Industrial development attracting migrants
• Small base population leading to higher percentage growth
• In-migration from neighboring states

• Option B is incorrect: Delhi had a growth rate of 20.9%
• Option C is incorrect: Puducherry had a growth rate of 28.1%
• Option D is incorrect: Chandigarh had a growth rate of 17.1%
The high growth in Dadra and Nagar Haveli was primarily due to industrial development and consequent in-migration

q.t:
Consider the following statements about internal migration in India:
1. Rural to urban migration constitutes the largest stream of internal migration in India
2. Marriage is the single largest cause of migration for females in India
3. Interstate migration rate has increased significantly after economic liberalization
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is incorrect: According to Census 2011 and NSS data, rural-to-rural migration constitutes the largest stream (about 53% of total migration), not rural-to-urban. This is primarily due to marriage-related migration of women from one rural area to another.
• Statement 2 is correct: Marriage accounts for approximately 46% of total migration and is overwhelmingly the primary cause for female migration (about 65% of female migrants cite marriage as the reason). This reflects traditional marriage patterns where women move to their husband's place.
• Statement 3 is incorrect: Interstate migration rate has actually remained relatively stable at around 4% of total population since 1991, despite economic liberalization. Most migration in India remains intrastate.

q.t: “Golden Rice” is a new type of rice developed through genetic engineering to produce beta carotene, in the edible parts of the rice. This modification aims to address:

so.t:
Golden Rice: The International Rice Research Institute (IRRI) and its national research partners created Golden Rice to supplement existing initiatives focused on addressing Vitamin A Deficiency (VAD), a major public health concern that affects millions of children and pregnant women worldwide.
● It has the potential to alleviate VAD in nations across South and Southeast Asia, where rice accounts for a significant share of daily calorie intake, particularly among communities largely reliant on rice for sustenance.
● It is intended to complement existing VAD prevention techniques, such as ingesting natural sources of vitamin A or beta-carotene, fortified meals, vitamin A supplements, and fostering optimum breastfeeding practices.
● In July 2021, the Philippines became the first country in the world to approve Golden Rice for commercial propagation.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
Relevance: Recently, a court in the Philippines overturned biosafety permits that had been granted for the commercial cultivation of genetically modified Golden Rice and Bt eggplant.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about migration patterns during the COVID-19 pandemic in India:
1. The pandemic led to the largest reverse migration in India since partition
2. Most reverse migrants during lockdown returned from Maharashtra, Gujarat, and Delhi to Uttar Pradesh and Bihar
3. Post-lockdown, the rate of return migration to urban centers exceeded pre-COVID levels
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is correct: The COVID-19 lockdown in March 2020 triggered massive reverse migration, with an estimated 10-14 crore migrant workers moving from urban to rural areas. This was indeed the largest internal migration movement since the 1947 partition, creating unprecedented humanitarian and logistical challenges.
• Statement 2 is correct: Major destination states like Maharashtra (especially Mumbai), Gujarat (Ahmedabad, Surat), Delhi, Karnataka (Bangalore), and Tamil Nadu (Chennai) witnessed massive outflow. Primary source states receiving reverse migrants were Uttar Pradesh (estimated 32 lakh returnees), Bihar (15 lakh), Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, and Jharkhand.
• Statement 3 is incorrect: Post-lockdown return migration to urban centers has been slower and incomplete. Many studies indicate that only 60-70% of migrants returned to their original destinations by late 2021. Factors include: • Fear of future lockdowns
• Better rural employment through MGNREGA expansion
• Improved rural infrastructure and digital connectivity
• Preference for staying closer to family

opt.t: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): The 2025 Human Development Report ranks India’s life expectancy at its highest since the inception of the HDI index.
Reason (R): India’s health infrastructure and post-pandemic recovery efforts have significantly improved life expectancy.

so.t:
• The report states India’s life expectancy reached 72 years in 2023, the highest since the HDI started.
• This improvement is credited to healthcare improvements and recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic impact.

opt.t: Increase in Gross National Income (GNI) per capita
Improvements in education indicators like mean and expected years of schooling
Better health outcomes, increasing life expectancy

q.t: Which of the following factors contributed to India's HDI improving by over 53% since 1990?

so.t: • All three factors—income rise, educational attainment improvements, and better health outcomes—combined have elevated India’s HDI significantly.

q.t: Which Indian state/union territory ranks highest on the Human Development Index as per the latest data?

so.t: • Goa ranks highest among Indian states/UTs with an HDI around 0.760, followed closely by Kerala and Chandigarh.

opt.t: It refers to a legal right granted to a party in a lawsuit to prevent the other party from selling or transferring the disputed property.
It allows the court to appoint a neutral third party to manage the disputed property until the case is resolved.
It ensures that any transfer of immovable property during pending litigation does not affect the rights of the parties involved.
It ensures that any legal action regarding immovable property must be settled before it can be transferred.

q.t: Which one of the following statements best describes the term “Doctrine of lis pendens”?

so.t:
● The “Doctrine of lis pendens” ensures that any transfer of immovable property during pending litigation does not affect the rights of the parties involved. It is intended to maintain the status quo and protect the interests of the parties until the dispute is resolved by the court. It prevents the transfer of property from complicating the legal process and affecting the outcome of the litigation.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
Relevance: The Supreme Court recently ruled that even if Section 52 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1881 (TPA) does not apply, the doctrine of lis pendens, based on justice, equity, and good conscience, still holds.

q.t:
Match the following Indian states/UTs with their approximate HDI category according to the HDI index:
State/UT
HDI Category
1. Goa
 A. High
2. Kerala
 B. Medium
3. Punjab
 C. Low
4. Bihar
Select the correct matching:

so.t:
• Goa and Kerala both have high HDI values (around 0.75+), reflecting better health, education, and income levels.
• Punjab is classified as medium human development (around 0.69).
• Bihar remains in the low HDI category, reflecting challenges in education, health, and income.

opt.t: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): Smart Cities Mission has accelerated the development of urban infrastructure but has shown limited impact on improving living conditions in informal settlements.
Reason (R): The mission primarily focuses on technology-driven solutions and area-based development rather than inclusive city-wide transformation.
In the context of recent evaluations, which of the following is correct?

so.t:
• Assertion A is true: Recent evaluations (2023-24) of Smart Cities Mission show: • ₹2.05 lakh crore investment across 100 cities has improved digital infrastructure, waste management, and transportation
• However, CSIR-CRRI and independent studies indicate limited direct benefit to slum dwellers and informal settlements
• Cities like Pune, Bhopal showed infrastructure improvement but persistent challenges in affordable housing and basic services for urban poor

• Reason R is true and correctly explains A: The mission's approach has been: • Area-Based Development (ABD): Focus on specific zones rather than city-wide coverage
• Technology emphasis: IoT, smart grids, digital governance
• Limited integration with schemes like PM-SVANidhi for street vendors or slum rehabilitation programs
• Insufficient focus on inclusive planning that addresses needs of informal sector workers

opt.t: Use emerging technologies like AI, blockchain, and drones for farm data collection and precision farming
Merge agriculture ministries with rural development

q.t: The Digital Agriculture Mission (2021-2025) aims to:

so.t:
• Objective: Leverage digital tech for farm advisory, input efficiency, yield forecasting, and traceability.
• Pilot projects include blockchain for seed supply chains and AI-based pest monitoring

q.t:
Which of the following statement/s is correct?
1. India is the largest producer of pulses globally.
2. Madhya Pradesh is the largest pulses-producing state in India.
3. Pulse productivity in India is above the world average.

so.t:
• 1: Correct – ~25% of global pulses output.
• 2: Correct – MP (“pulse bowl of India”) leads in tur, urad, masoor.
• 3: Incorrect – India’s average ~800 kg/ha vs global average ~900+ kg/ha (FAO).

q.t:
Consider the following:
1. Vice President
2. Cabinet Minister
3. Senior citizens aged 85+
4. Paramilitary forces
5. Persons employed in essential services
How many of the above are eligible to be “Absentee voters” under the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961?

so.t:
● The Representation of the People Act, 1951 (RPA) generally requires voters to cast their ballots in person at their designated polling station, during the specified date and hours, using Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs). However, to ensure inclusivity and allow every eligible voter to exercise their franchise, special exceptions have been made for certain categories of voters. These categories are permitted to use alternative methods of voting. Following are the alternative voting methods and the groups eligible for them:
● ‘Postal ballot’ allows voters who cannot be physically present in polling stations to vote remotely, as specified in the RPA, 1951. This method differs from normal voting in three ways.
○ Polling takes place outside the polling station;
○ It takes place without EVMs;
○ Polling takes place before the designated poll date in the constituency.
○ As per the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961, the following classes of persons are entitled to vote by postal ballot:
■ Special voters: Individuals holding declared office under Section 20(4) of RPA, including the President, Vice President, Governors, Cabinet Ministers, other high-ranking dignitaries, etc. and their spouses. So, points 1 and 2 are not correct.
■ Service voters: Members of the Indian armed forces, paramilitary forces, an armed state police member serving outside their state, or a government employee stationed abroad and their spouses residing with them. So, point 4 is not correct.
■ Voters on election duty: This includes all Commission’s observers, Presiding officers, polling officers and agents, police personnel, and public servants assigned official tasks on polling day. Private individuals and non-government staff, such as videographers, control room staff, drivers, conductors, cleaners, helpline staff, etc., are also covered.
■ Electors subjected to preventive detention.
○ Absentee voters under Section 60 (c) of RPA, 1951: In 2019, the Election Commission created the ‘Absentee Voters’ category. This includes senior citizens aged 85+ (AVSC), persons with disabilities having at least 40% disability (AVPD), COVID-19 suspect or affected persons (AVCO), and persons employed in essential services (AVES). So, points 3 and 5 are correct.
■ AVES includes officials certified to be on duty on the day of the poll but not on election duty, including railways, telecom, electricity, health, traffic, aviation, fire services, media persons authorised by ECI for poll day coverage, etc.
So, only two of the above are eligible to be “Absentee voters” under the Conduct of Election Rules, 1961. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

q.t: Which crop in India is most sensitive to temperature rise due to climate change, leading to reduced yield potential?

so.t:
Wheat is highly temperature-sensitive; a 1°C rise during grain filling reduces yields by 4–5%.
• 2022 & 2023 heatwaves in North India cut wheat output, triggering export bans.

q.t:
Arrange the following in chronological order:
1. Introduction of high-yielding wheat varieties in Punjab-Haryana
2. Launch of National Food Security Mission
3. Second phase of Green Revolution in Eastern India
4. Start of Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana

so.t:
• HYV wheat – mid-1960s
• NFSM – 2007
• Second Green Revolution (Eastern India) – announced in 2010-11 budget
• PMKSY – launched 2015.

q.t:
Crop
 Major producing state
1. Jowar
 A. West Bengal
2. Tea
 B. Maharashtra
3. Makhana
 C. Bihar
Select the correct match:

so.t:
• Jowar – Maharashtra & Karnataka
• Tea – Assam & West Bengal
• Makhana – Bihar (over 80% of India’s output)
• Recent GI tagging for Mithila Makhana boosted exports in 2023.

q.t:
Which of the following statement/s is correct?
1. Black soils are best suited for cotton cultivation due to their high water retention and nutrient content.
2. In India, black soils are mostly found in the Indo-Gangetic plains.

so.t:
1: Correct – Black (regur) soils retain moisture, rich in calcium carbonate & potassium, ideal for cotton.
• 2: Incorrect – Found mainly in Deccan Plateau (Maharashtra, MP, Gujarat, AP, Karnataka), not the Indo-Gangetic plain.

q.t:
Which of the following atmospheric conditions can result in turbulence in an airplane?
1. Eddies caused because of irregular terrain
2. Convective currents with rising warm air
3. Friction between the opposing air masses
4. Changes in wind direction over a specific vertical distance
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
Turbulence is one of the most unpredictable of all the weather phenomena that are of significance to pilots. It is an irregular motion of the air resulting from eddies and vertical currents. It is associated with fronts, wind shear, thunderstorms, etc.
● The following are the causes of turbulence in an airplane:
○ Mechanical Turbulence:
■ Caused by friction between the air and the ground, especially due to irregular terrain and man-made obstacles.
■ In strong winds, even hangars and large buildings can cause eddies that are carried some distance downwind. So, point 1 is correct.
○ Thermal (Convective) Turbulence:
■ Found during the day, especially on warm summer days when the sun heats the Earth's surface unevenly.
■ Surfaces like barren ground and rocky and sandy areas heat more rapidly than grass-covered fields and water.
■ Isolated convective currents with warm air rising and cooler air descending create bumpy conditions as an airplane flies through them. So, point 2 is correct.
○ Frontal Turbulence:
■ This is caused by the lifting of warm air by the sloping frontal surface and friction between the opposing air masses in the frontal zone. So, point 3 is correct.
■ Most are marked when the warm air is moist and unstable, and extremely severe if thunderstorms develop.
■ More commonly associated with cold fronts but can also be present, to a lesser degree, in warm fronts.
○ Wind Shear Turbulence:
■ Caused by changes in wind direction and/or wind speed over a specific horizontal or vertical distance. So, point 4 is correct.
■ It occurs in areas with temperature inversions, along troughs and lows, and around jet streams.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's land degradation status as per the latest UNCCD Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN) report 2024:
1. Rajasthan accounts for the highest area under land degradation in India
2. Soil erosion affects approximately 30% of India's total geographical area
3. India has achieved net positive land restoration under the LDN framework
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is correct: According to the 2024 UNCCD report and ICFRE data, Rajasthan has the largest area under land degradation (approximately 9.06 million hectares), primarily due to desertification, salinity, and water logging.
• Statement 2 is correct: ISRO's Desertification and Land Degradation Atlas 2021 (updated 2024) shows that soil erosion affects about 29.32% of India's total geographical area (96.40 million hectares out of 328.72 million hectares).
• Statement 3 is incorrect: While India has made progress in land restoration (Bonn Challenge commitment of 26 million hectares by 2030), it has not yet achieved net positive restoration under LDN. Current restoration rate is slower than degradation rate in several regions.
Current initiatives: PM-KUSUM for solar agriculture, Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) expansion, and the National Mission on Sustainable Agriculture are addressing land degradation

opt.t: Complete elimination of chemical fertilizers in target districts
Focus on zero-budget natural farming with cow-based preparations
Integration of organic farming with modern precision agriculture
Replacement of traditional seeds with genetically modified varieties

q.t: Which of the following best describes the impact of Natural Farming initiatives promoted under Bharatiya Prakritik Krishi Paddhati (BPKP) launched in 2024-25?

so.t:
• Option B is correct: BPKP (launched in Budget 2024-25) promotes zero-budget natural farming based on: • Cow-based preparations (Jivamrita, Beejamrita, Ghanjeevamrita)
• Elimination of external inputs including chemical fertilizers and pesticides
• Traditional farming practices combined with scientific validation
• Coverage initially in 15,000 clusters across all states

• Option A is incorrect: BPKP aims for gradual transition, not immediate complete elimination of chemicals.
• Option C is incorrect: While modern techniques are used for monitoring, the core philosophy opposes precision agriculture's input-intensive approach.
• Option D is incorrect: BPKP promotes indigenous and traditional seed varieties, not GM crops.
Current status (2024-25):
• ₹2,481 crore allocated over 3 years
• Focus states: Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Himachal Pradesh showing early adoption
• Integration with PM-KISAN for farmer support

opt.t: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): The implementation of e-NAM (National Agriculture Market) platform has significantly reduced price volatility for farmers across India.
Reason (R): e-NAM provides real-time price discovery and eliminates intermediaries between farmers and buyers.
Based on recent performance data (2023-24), which of the following is correct?

so.t:
• Assertion A is false: According to 2023-24 evaluation reports: • e-NAM has had limited impact on price volatility reduction
• Only 25% of participating mandis show significant price stabilization
• Structural issues like inadequate storage, transportation bottlenecks persist
• Farmers still face price fluctuations due to seasonal gluts and supply chain inefficiencies

• Reason R is true: e-NAM platform does provide: • Real-time price information across 1,361 mandis
• Direct interface between farmers and buyers
• Elimination of some intermediary layers
• Quality testing and online payment systems

• Why R doesn't explain A: While e-NAM provides price transparency, it hasn't addressed fundamental issues: • Inadequate rural infrastructure
• Lack of farmer awareness and digital literacy
• Dominance of traditional mandi systems
• Insufficient post-harvest management facilities

opt.t: Insurance coverage has reached 100% of eligible farmers across all states
Claim settlement ratio has improved significantly with technology integration
Premium burden on farmers has been completely eliminated
Crop loss assessment is now entirely satellite-based without field verification

q.t: Which of the following statements about the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana’s (PMFBY) implementation is most accurate?

so.t:
• Option A is incorrect: Coverage remains around 30% of eligible farmers (approximately 5.5 crore farmers enrolled in 2023-24), with significant interstate variations. States like Punjab, Haryana show higher coverage while northeastern states lag behind.
• Option B is correct: Technology integration has improved performance: • Claim settlement ratio increased from 67% (2020-21) to 78% (2023-24)
• Satellite imagery, drones, and smartphone apps for damage assessment
• Reduced settlement time from 6-8 months to 3-4 months on average
• PMFBY portal integration with land records and bank accounts

• Option C is incorrect: Farmers pay 2% premium for kharif, 1.5% for rabi crops (up to maximum of ₹75,000 sum insured). Government subsidizes the balance premium.
• Option D is incorrect: While satellite technology is extensively used, field verification remains mandatory for localized calamities and quality assurance. CCEs (Crop Cutting Experiments) continue alongside remote sensing.
Current challenges: Weather station density, awareness among small farmers, and timely premium collection remain issues.

q.t:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Financial Services Institutions Bureau (FSIB):
1. Its chairperson is nominated by the Reserve Bank of India.
2. It has been established under the Department of Economic Affairs, Ministry of Finance.
3. It appoints whole-time directors for senior positions in Public Sector Banks (PSBs).
How many of the above statements are correct?

so.t:
Financial Services Institutions Bureau (FSIB):
● It has replaced the Banks Board Bureau (BBB) to select the chiefs of public sector banks and insurance companies.
○ BBB was originally set up in 2016 to select the CEOs and Executive Directors of public sector banks and the chiefs of insurance companies.
● It comprises a chairperson nominated by the Central Government; the secretaries of the departments of financial services and public enterprises; the chairman of the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India; and a deputy governor of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). So, statement 1 is not correct.
● It is a professional body under the Department of Financial Services, Ministry of Finance, with autonomy in its affairs and has its own secretariat. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● Composition of FSIB:
● FSIB is the single entity for making recommendations for the appointments of whole-time directors and non-executive chairpersons of:
○ Public Sector Banks (PSBs)
○ Financial Institutions (FIs)
○ Public-Sector Insurance (PSI) companies.
● FSIB would recommend the name and the final decision would be taken by the Appointments Committee of the Cabinet headed by the Prime Minister of India. So, statement 3 is not correct.
● Additionally, it has three part-time members who are experts in banking and three more from the insurance sector.
So, none of the statements given above are correct.
Other provisions of the Financial Services Institutions Bureau (FSIB):
● It also advises the government on a suitable performance appraisal system for whole-time directors and non-executive chairmen of the state-run financial services institutions.
● It builds a data bank relating to the performance of Public-Sector Banks (PSBs), FIs and insurance companies.
● It advises the government on the “formulation and enforcement of a code of conduct and ethics for whole-time directors” in these institutions.
● It helps state-run banks, Financial Institutions (FIs) and insurers in developing business strategies and capital raising plans, etc.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's agricultural export performance in 2023-24:
1. India became the world's largest exporter of rice, surpassing Thailand
2. Export restrictions on wheat and rice were completely lifted by March 2024
3. Marine products exports declined due to antibiotic residue issues in international markets
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is correct: India became the world's largest rice exporter in 2023-24 with approximately 22 million tonnes (22.7% of global exports), surpassing Thailand (9.8 million tonnes). This despite selective export restrictions on certain varieties.
• Statement 2 is incorrect: Export restrictions remain in place as of 2024: • Rice: Ban on non-basmati white rice continues (except for humanitarian purposes)
• Wheat: Export restrictions remain with exceptions for neighboring countries and humanitarian aid
• These measures aim to ensure domestic food security and price stability

• Statement 3 is correct: Marine products exports faced challenges: • EU and US rejections due to antibiotic residue detection
• Export value declined from $8.09 billion (2021-22) to $7.76 billion (2023-24)
• Shrimp exports particularly affected, leading to enhanced quality control measures

Current policy response:
• Strengthened quality testing infrastructure
• MPEDA initiatives for residue-free aquaculture
• Diversification to new markets like Southeast Asia

q.t:
Consider the following statements about the Minimum Support Price (MSP) policy and its recent developments in 2024-25:
1. MSP is currently announced for 23 crops including cereals, pulses, oilseeds, and commercial crops
2. The government has achieved the target of fixing MSP at 1.5 times the production cost (A2+FL) for all announced crops
3. Procurement at MSP covers less than 10% of total production for most crops except rice and wheat
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is correct: MSP is announced for 23 crops: • Cereals (7): Paddy, wheat, jowar, bajra, maize, barley, ragi
• Pulses (5): Arhar, moong, urad, lentil, gram
• Oilseeds (8): Groundnut, sunflower, soybean, sesame, safflower, niger, mustard, coconut
• Commercial crops (3): Cotton, sugarcane, jute

• Statement 2 is incorrect (This is the tricky part): While government claims MSP is 1.5 times A2+FL cost: • A2+FL includes paid-out costs plus imputed family labor but excludes land rent
• Many economists argue for C2 cost (comprehensive cost including land rent) consideration
• For crops like arhar, moong, urad - MSP in 2024-25 is below 1.5 times C2 cost
• Example: Arhar MSP 2024-25 is ₹7,550/quintal while C2 cost is ₹5,755/quintal (ratio 1.31)
• The "achievement" depends on which cost calculation method is used

• Statement 3 is correct: Procurement reality: • Rice and wheat: 35-40% of production procured at MSP
• Pulses: 5-8% procurement at MSP
• Oilseeds: 2-5% procurement at MSP
• Cotton: 15-20% through Cotton Corporation of India
• Most farmers sell in open market at prices often below MSP

Current challenges:
• Storage and logistics constraints for pulse/oilseed procurement
• Private trade reluctance to match MSP prices
• Regional variations in procurement infrastructure

opt.t: Groundwater levels have improved uniformly across all states due to good monsoons
Over-exploitation of groundwater continues in 1,186 blocks, with Punjab showing the highest stress
Artificial recharge structures under MGNREGA have successfully recharged 50% of depleted aquifers
Groundwater quality has improved significantly due to reduced industrial pollution

q.t: Which of the following statements about India's groundwater scenario as per the Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) assessment is most appropriate?

so.t:
• Option A is incorrect: Groundwater improvement has been uneven: • Southern states like Tamil Nadu, Karnataka showed improvement
• Northwestern states (Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan) continue to face severe depletion
• Eastern states have relatively stable levels
• Monsoon impact varies regionally and doesn't guarantee uniform recovery

• Option B is correct: CGWB 2024 data shows: • 1,186 blocks categorized as over-exploited (extraction >100% of annual replenishment)
• Punjab has 79% of its blocks over-exploited (highest percentage)
• Haryana (65%), Delhi (100%), Rajasthan (51%) also critically stressed
• Major concerns in Indo-Gangetic plains due to intensive agriculture

• Option C is incorrect: While MGNREGA structures contribute to recharge: • Impact assessment shows limited success in severely depleted areas
• 50% aquifer recharge claim is not substantiated by scientific studies
• Effectiveness varies greatly based on geological conditions

• Option D is incorrect: Groundwater quality challenges persist: • Fluoride contamination in Rajasthan, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh
• Arsenic contamination in West Bengal, Bihar
• Nitrate pollution from fertilizer use remains widespread

q.t:
Consider the following statements about the National River Conservation Programme (NRCP) and its recent performance:
1. The Namami Gange programme has achieved significant reduction in biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) levels in the Ganga river
2. Real-time water quality monitoring systems have been installed at over 1,000 locations across major rivers
3. River rejuvenation efforts have been successful in bringing back the extinct Gangetic dolphin population to pre-1980 levels
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is correct: Namami Gange achievements (2024 assessment): • BOD levels reduced from 40-50 mg/l to 3-4 mg/l at several monitoring stations
• Dissolved oxygen levels improved from 4-5 mg/l to 7-8 mg/l in stretches
• 378 Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) constructed/under construction
• Industrial effluent monitoring strengthened with real-time systems

• Statement 2 is correct: Water quality monitoring expansion: • 1,275+ real-time monitoring stations installed across major rivers
• Automatic monitoring of pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, conductivity
• Integration with Central Pollution Control Board database
• Mobile app-based reporting system for public access

• Statement 3 is incorrect: Gangetic dolphin population status: • Current population estimated at 4,000-5,000 (2024 survey)
• Pre-1980 population was estimated at 15,000-20,000
• While population has stabilized and shown marginal increase from 2010 levels
• Population is still far below historical levels due to habitat fragmentation

Recent initiatives:
• Ganga Task Force 2.0 for enforcement
• Biodiversity conservation projects including dolphin sanctuaries
• Community participation through Ganga Praharis programme

q.t:
With reference to the Reserve Bank of India’s gold loan norms, consider the following statements:
1. Lenders cannot lend more than 75% of the value of the gold.
2. No gold loan money can be disbursed in the form of cash.
3. Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) cannot lend against gold as collateral.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) recently emphasized the need for gold loan lenders to adhere strictly to regulatory norms. This move aims to tighten oversight over Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) following instances of regulatory non-compliance.
● The RBI stipulates lenders to comply with certain norms while lending money in lieu of gold. For instance, lenders are not allowed to lend any amount of money that is greater than 75% of the value of the gold that is submitted as collateral by the borrower. So, statement 1 is correct.
○ This is to ensure that banks have sufficient cushion to absorb any losses by selling the gold in case the borrower defaults on the loan.
● In order to comply with income tax rules, the RBI also mandates that when a loan is disbursed to a borrower, no more than ₹20,000 can be disbursed in the form of cash; the remaining loan amount needs to be deposited in the borrower’s bank account. It also instructs lenders to conduct the auction of any gold (in case a borrower defaults) fairly and transparently in locations that are accessible to the borrowers. So, statement 2 is not correct.
● NBFCs are allowed to lend against gold as collateral. The NBFCs expect the RBI’s scrutiny of their lending practices to affect their growth and profitability. So, statement 3 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
Non-Banking Financial Company (NBFC):
● It is a company registered under the Companies Act, 1956.
● It is engaged in the business of loans and advances and the acquisition of shares/stocks/bonds/debentures/securities issued by the Government or local authority.
● It cannot accept demand deposits;
● It does not form part of the payment and settlement system and cannot issue cheques drawn on itself;
● The deposit insurance facility of Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation is not available to depositors of NBFCs, unlike in the case of banks.

opt.t: The project has been completed and is fully operational for irrigation and power generation
Construction is ongoing with 90% physical progress, but faces displacement and environmental challenges
The project has been shelved due to interstate water disputes with Odisha and Chhattisgarh
Only the irrigation component is functional while power generation remains non-operational

q.t: Which of the following best describes the current status of the Polavaram Multi-Purpose Project in Andhra Pradesh as of 2024?

so.t:
• Option A is incorrect: Project is not fully operational yet, though spillway and some gates are functional.
• Option B is correct: Current status (2024): • Physical progress approximately 90% (main dam structure largely complete)
• Spillway and some gates operational for flood management
• Key challenges remain: • Displacement of approximately 3 lakh people from submerged villages
• Environmental clearance issues for increased reservoir height
• Resettlement and rehabilitation delays
• Forest clearance complications


• Option C is incorrect: While there are interstate concerns, the project hasn't been shelved: • Godavari River Management Board coordinates interstate issues
• Odisha and Chhattisgarh raised concerns about backwater effects
• Central government mediating through technical committees

• Option D is incorrect: Neither irrigation nor power generation is fully operational yet, though testing phases have begun for some components.

q.t:
Which of the following is correct?
1. The Indus Waters Treaty (1960) grants exclusive use of Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej waters to Pakistan.
2. The Bhakra Nangal Dam primarily utilises Sutlej river water for irrigation and hydropower.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?

so.t:
• Statement 1 is incorrect — under the Indus Waters Treaty, Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej are allocated to India, while Indus, Jhelum, Chenab go to Pakistan.
• Statement 2 is correct — Bhakra Nangal Dam on Sutlej is a major multi-purpose project serving irrigation and hydropower in Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan.

q.t:
Arrange the following in order of year of their completion
1. Hirakud Dam
2. Sardar Sarovar Dam
3. Nagarjuna Sagar Dam
4. Indira Gandhi Canal (Phase I completion)

so.t:
• Hirakud Dam – 1957
• Nagarjuna Sagar – 1967
• Indira Gandhi Canal Phase I – 1983
• Sardar Sarovar Dam – 2017 final height completion (though partial operation began earlier).

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's population distribution:
1. The Northern Plains account for about 40% of India's population despite covering only 25% of the geographical area.
2. West Bengal has the highest population density among all Indian states.
3. The population density of India as per Census 2011 is 382 persons per sq km.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Statement 1 is CORRECT: The Northern Plains (Indo-Gangetic Plains) indeed support about 40% of India's population while covering approximately 25% of the geographical area due to fertile alluvial soil, adequate water supply, and favorable climate.
• Statement 2 is INCORRECT: Bihar has the highest population density (1106 persons per sq km as per Census 2011), not West Bengal (1028 persons per sq km). This is a common misconception as West Bengal was the leader in earlier censuses.
• Statement 3 is CORRECT: As per Census 2011, India's population density is 382 persons per sq km, increased from 325 in 2001

q.t:
With reference to the rangelands, consider the following statements:
1. They are primarily used for cultivating crops due to their fertile soil.
2. They can help in reducing the frequency of dust storms.
3. Grazing by domestic livestock on rangelands can help to reduce the risk of wildfires.
Which of the statements given above are correct?

so.t:
Rangelands:
● They are uncultivated grasslands, shrublands, woodlands, wetlands and savannas that are suitable for grazing and browsing domestic and wild animals. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● Sustainable management of rangelands contributes as carbon sinks to mitigate the changing climate and reduce the dust storms frequency. So, statement 2 is correct.
● Domestic livestock grazing often reduces fine fuels which if left unchecked creates a greater risk of wildfire. Some grazing by domestic livestock protects important habitats for rare and endangered flora and fauna species. So, statement 3 is correct.
● In India, rangelands occupy about 121 million hectares and the area used for grazing is estimated at around 40 per cent of the total land surface of India, including grasslands (17 per cent), and forests (23 per cent).
● They provide renewable resources such as clean water and wind for energy production and nonrenewable resources like oil, coal, and other minerals.

opt.t: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

q.t: Assertion (A): The southern states of India show lower birth rates compared to northern states. Reason (R): Southern states have achieved higher levels of female literacy and better healthcare infrastructure.

so.t:
Assertion is CORRECT: Southern states like Kerala (14.8), Tamil Nadu (15.7), Karnataka (18.2) have significantly lower birth rates per 1000 population compared to northern states like Bihar (27.6), Uttar Pradesh (27.2), Rajasthan (25.4).
Reason is CORRECT and properly explains the assertion: Higher female literacy rates in southern states (Kerala 92%, Tamil Nadu 73.4% vs UP 59.3%, Bihar 53.3%) correlate with delayed marriages, family planning awareness, and reduced fertility rates. Better healthcare infrastructure ensures effective family planning services

q.t:
Which of the following statements about India's coal reserves and production is/are correct?
1. Jharia coalfield in Jharkhand is known for coking coal production.
2. Odisha is the largest coal-producing state in India.
3. India has the world's fifth-largest proven coal reserves.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
Statement 1 is CORRECT: Jharia coalfield is India's most important coking coal producer, essential for steel industry. It produces high-grade bituminous coal suitable for metallurgical purposes.
Statement 2 is CORRECT: Odisha leads in coal production with approximately 24% of India's total coal output, mainly from fields like Talcher, Ib Valley, and Mahanadi basin.
Statement 3 is INCORRECT: India has the world's 4th largest proven coal reserves (after USA, Russia, and China), not fifth. Australia ranks fifth.

opt.t: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): India imports crude oil from the Middle East despite having significant domestic reserves.
Reason (R): India's domestic crude oil production meets only about 15% of its total petroleum requirements.

so.t:
Assertion is CORRECT: India imports approximately 85% of its crude oil requirements, with major suppliers being Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Iran, UAE, and Kuwait from the Middle East region.
Reason is CORRECT and explains the assertion: India's domestic crude oil production from fields like Mumbai High, Assam, Gujarat meets only about 15% of total consumption. Growing energy demand due to economic development far exceeds domestic production capacity.

q.t:
Consider the following mineral-location pairs:
1. Bauxite - Kalahandi (Odisha)
2. Iron ore - Bailadila (Chhattisgarh)
3. Manganese - Nagpur (Maharashtra)
4. Copper - Khetri (Rajasthan)
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?

so.t:
Pair 1 is CORRECT: Kalahandi district in Odisha is a major bauxite-producing region, part of the East Coast Bauxite belt.
Pair 2 is CORRECT: Bailadila in Chhattisgarh (Dantewada district) is one of India's richest iron ore deposits with high-grade ore (65-67% Fe content).
Pair 3 is INCORRECT: Nagpur region produces manganese, but the major manganese belt in Maharashtra is around Bhandara-Gondia region, not specifically Nagpur city.
Pair 4 is CORRECT: Khetri in Rajasthan (Jhunjhunu district) is India's largest copper mine and smelting complex

opt.t: Increase in iron-rich dust carried by strong winds
Sediment runoff due to increased deforestation activities
Release of toxic metals from thawing permafrost
Increased volcanic activity in the region

q.t: Which one of the following is causing the rivers and streams in the Arctic range to appear orange over the past five to ten years?

so.t:
According to a recent study, rivers and streams in Alaska have been changing colour from a clear blue to a rusty orange due to
toxic metals released by thawing permafrost.
● The researchers discovered that discolouration and cloudiness are caused by metals such as iron, zinc, copper, nickel, and lead.
These metals, some of which are toxic to river and stream ecosystems, are released as permafrost thaws and exposes
waterways to minerals that have been locked underground for thousands of years.
● Arctic soils naturally contain organic carbon, nutrients and metals, such as mercury, within their permafrost. High temperatures have caused these minerals and the water sources around them to meet as permafrost melts.
● This discolouration has been linked to “dramatic declines” in aquatic life, raising concerns about how the continued melting of permafrost will affect communities that rely on those waterways for drinking and fishing.
Permafrost:
● It is any ground that remains completely frozen—32°F (0°C) or colder—for at least two years straight. These permanently frozen grounds are most common in regions with high mountains and in Earth’s higher latitudes—near the North and South Poles.
● It covers large regions of the Earth. Almost a quarter of the land area in the Northern Hemisphere has permafrost underneath. Although the ground is frozen, permafrost regions are
not always covered in snow.
● It is made of a combination of soil, rocks and sand that are held together by ice. The soil and ice in permafrost stay frozen all year long.
● Near the surface, permafrost soils also contain large quantities of organic carbon—a material leftover from dead plants that couldn’t decompose, or rot away, due to the cold. Lower permafrost layers contain soils made mostly of minerals.

q.t:
Which of the following factors explains the concentration of nuclear power plants in peninsular India?
1. Availability of nuclear fuel deposits
2. Geological stability of the peninsula
3. Proximity to major industrial centers
4. Adequate water supply for cooling
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
Factor 2 is CORRECT: Peninsular India's geological stability (Deccan Plateau) with ancient Precambrian rocks provides earthquake-resistant foundation essential for nuclear installations.
Factor 4 is CORRECT: Nuclear plants require enormous amounts of water for cooling. Coastal locations (Kudankulam, Tarapur, Kaiga) and river-side locations ensure adequate water supply.
Factor 1 is INCORRECT: India's uranium deposits are mainly in Jharkhand (Jadugoda), Telangana (Lambapur), not necessarily in peninsular India where most plants are located.
Factor 3 is PARTIALLY CORRECT but not the primary reason: While proximity to demand centers matters, safety and geological factors take precedence in site selection.

q.t:
The recent discovery of lithium deposits in Jammu & Kashmir is significant for India because:
1. It reduces dependence on imports for battery manufacturing
2. It supports the electric vehicle revolution
3. Lithium is essential for renewable energy storage
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
All statements are CORRECT:
Statement 1: India currently imports 100% of its lithium requirements primarily from Australia, Chile, and Argentina. Domestic production would reduce this strategic dependence.
Statement 2: Lithium-ion batteries are crucial for electric vehicles. With India's push for 30% EV adoption by 2030, domestic lithium supports this transition.
Statement 3: Grid-scale energy storage using lithium batteries is essential for managing intermittent renewable energy from solar and wind power.
Geological context: The Reasi district discovery in J&K contains an estimated 5.9 million tonnes of lithium reserves, making India potentially self-sufficient

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's renewable energy sector:
1. India ranks 4th globally in total renewable energy capacity.
2. Rajasthan leads in solar power generation due to maximum solar irradiation.
3. Tamil Nadu has the highest wind power capacity among Indian states.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Statement 1 is CORRECT: India ranks 4th globally in total renewable energy capacity (after China, USA, Brazil) with over 175 GW installed capacity as of 2024.
Statement 2 is INCORRECT: While Rajasthan receives high solar irradiation, Tamil Nadu leads in total solar power installed capacity, not Rajasthan. Gujarat also has significant solar capacity.
Statement 3 is CORRECT: Tamil Nadu has the highest wind power capacity (~10.5 GW) due to favorable wind corridors, especially during southwest monsoon.
Additional context:
• India's renewable target: 500 GW by 2030
• Wind energy contribution: ~87 GW (Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka leading)
• Solar energy: ~75 GW installed capacity

opt.t: Limited domestic fossil fuel reserves and growing energy demand
Lack of technological capability in renewable energy sector
Geographic concentration of energy resources in few states

q.t: Which of the following best explains India's energy security challenges?

so.t:
Option (A) is CORRECT: India faces a classic energy security dilemma:
• Limited domestic reserves: Only 15% crude oil self-sufficiency, declining natural gas production
• Growing demand: 3-4% annual energy demand growth driven by economic development
• Import dependence: 85% crude oil imports, increasing coal imports despite domestic reserves
• Geopolitical risks: Dependence on politically unstable regions (Middle East)

q.t:
Match the following:
Dam
 River
 Primary Purpose
A. Tehri
 1. Narmada
 P. Irrigation
B. Sardar Sarovar
 2. Bhagirathi
 Q. Hydropower
C. Hirakud
 3. Mahanadi
 R. Flood Control

so.t:
Correct matching:
• Tehri Dam (A) is on Bhagirathi River (2) with primary purpose of Hydropower generation (Q) - 2400 MW capacity
• Sardar Sarovar Dam (B) is on Narmada River (1) with primary purpose of Irrigation (P) - irrigates 1.8 million hectares across Gujarat, MP, Maharashtra, Rajasthan
• Hirakud Dam (C) is on Mahanadi River (3) with primary purpose of Flood Control (R) - though it also serves irrigation and power generation
Technical details:
• Tehri: World's 10th highest dam at 260m
• Sardar Sarovar: India's largest dam by volume
• Hirakud: One of India's first multipurpose projects (1957)

opt.t: Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct, but Statement–II does not explain Statement–I
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is incorrect
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I:
Chir Pine trees are highly inflammable and prone to forest fires.
Statement-II:
Chir Pine trees have resin-rich needle leaves and twigs.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

so.t:
Chir Pine trees, also known as Pinus roxburghii, are known for their high flammability and contribution to forest fires in the Himalayas and other regions. So, statement 1 is correct.
● Chir Pine trees have needle-like leaves with a high resin content. Resin is a flammable substance that can easily ignite and spread fire. So, statement 2 is correct.
● The resin-rich nature of Chir Pine trees, which includes their leaves and twigs, makes them highly flammable. Resin can easily ignite and sustain fire, contributing to the trees' overall susceptibility to forest fires.
Hence, the inflammability and proneness of forest fires of Chir Pine trees can be directly attributed to their resin rich composition.
Chir Pine:
● Native to the Himalayas, Chir Pine, also known as the longleaf Indian pine, was not introduced to the region by the British, contrary to popular belief.
● It is widespread throughout the Western Himalayas, thriving at elevations between 1,000 and 2,000 meters. It covers extensive areas in Garhwal and Kumaon, often seen growing on steep
slopes. The tree is distinguishable by its three needles and round cones, in contrast to the elongated cones of other pine species.

q.t: The 'Golden Quadrilateral' of mineral resources in India refers to the region connecting:

so.t:
Option (A) is CORRECT: The Golden Quadrilateral of minerals refers to the iron-steel belt connecting:
• Ranchi: Administrative center with mineral wealth
• Rourkela: Major steel plant location (RSP)
• Durgapur: Steel plant and heavy industries
• Jamshedpur: Tata Steel, first steel plant in India

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's natural gas scenario:
1. India's largest natural gas reserves are located in the Krishna-Godavari basin.
2. Reliance Industries operates India's largest private sector natural gas production.
3. India is a net exporter of natural gas.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
Statement 1 is CORRECT: Krishna-Godavari (KG) basin, particularly KG-D6 block, contains India's largest discovered natural gas reserves, estimated at over 10 trillion cubic feet.
Statement 2 is CORRECT: Reliance Industries, through its KG-D6 block operations, is India's largest private sector natural gas producer, contributing significantly to domestic production.
Statement 3 is INCORRECT: India is a net importer of natural gas. Domestic production meets only about 50% of demand, with imports through pipelines and LNG terminals making up the deficit.
Additional context:
• Major import sources: Qatar, Australia, USA (LNG)
• Pipeline imports: Myanmar
• Growing demand for cleaner fuel transitions

opt.t: Proximity to iron and steel plants
Availability of skilled labor and technical institutions
Access to major ports for export

q.t: Which of the following factors best explains the concentration of automobile industry in Chennai-Hosur-Bangalore belt?

so.t:
Option (B) is CORRECT: The Chennai-Hosur-Bangalore automobile corridor succeeded due to:
• Technical institutions: IITs, IISc Bangalore, Anna University producing skilled engineers
• Skilled workforce: Traditional mechanical skills, adaptable to modern manufacturing
• Knowledge ecosystem: R&D centers, design studios, technical support services
• Educational infrastructure: Engineering colleges supplying talent pipeline

q.t:
With reference to the National Savings Certificate (NSC) Scheme, which of the following statements are correct?
1. The account can be opened by both citizens and non-citizens.
2. There is no maximum limit nor a limit on the number of deposits.
3. NSC can be transferred to another person on the death of the account holder only.
4. The certificates are accepted as collateral for secured loans in banks and NBFCs.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
National Savings Certificate (NSC) Scheme:
● It is a government-backed fixed-income investment scheme offered by India Post. It attracts conservative investors making medium to small-scale investments for five years. The interest rates for NSC are pre-determined and reviewed/revised by the Ministry of Finance quarterly.
● The following are the features of the National Savings Certificate (NSC) Scheme:
● Who can open:
○ a single adult
○ joint account (up to 3 adults)
○ a guardian on behalf of a minor or on behalf of a person of unsound mind
○ a minor above 10 years in his own name.
○ s/he must be a citizen of India. So, statement 1 is not correct.
● Deposit:
○ Minimum Rs. 1000 and in multiple of Rs. 100, no maximum limit.
○ Any number of accounts can be opened under the scheme. Hence it has no maximum limit nor a limit on the number of deposits. So, statement 2 is correct.
○ Deposits qualify for deduction under section 80C of the Income Tax Act (1961).
● Transfer of account from one person to another person:
○ NSC may be transferred from one person to another person on the following conditions only:
■ On the death of the account holder to nominee/legal heirs.
■ On the death of the account holder to the joint holder(s).
■ On order by the court.
■ On pledging of account to the specified authority. So, statement 3 is not correct.
● Loan Collateral:
○ Most banks and NBFCs accept NSC certificates as collateral or security deposits if an investor is opting for a secured loan. In such instances, banks or post offices attach a transfer stamp to this certificate and transfer it to the bank or NBFC which will disburse the loan. So, statement 4 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
Other provisions of the National Savings Certificate (NSC) Scheme:
● Maturity:
o The deposit shall mature on completion of five years from the date of the deposit.
● Pledging of account:
o NSC may be pledged or transferred as security, by submitting a prescribed application form at the concerned Post Office supported with an acceptance letter from the pledgee.
o Transfer/pledging can be made to the following authorities:
▪ The President of India/Governor of the State.
▪ RBI/Scheduled Bank/Co-operative Society/Co-operative Bank.
▪ Corporation (public/private)/Govt. Company/Local Authority.
o Housing finance company.
● Premature closure:
o NSC may not be prematurely closed before 5 years except for the following conditions:
▪ On the death of a single account, or any or all the account holders in a joint account
▪ On forfeiture by a pledgee being a Gazetted officer.
▪ On order by court.

q.t:
Consider the following statements about India's textile industry:
1. Cotton textile production is concentrated in Maharashtra and Gujarat due to cotton cultivation.
2. Jute industry is declining due to competition from synthetic substitutes.
3. India is the world's second-largest textile exporter after China.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?

so.t:
All statements are CORRECT:
Statement 1: Maharashtra (Mumbai, Pune, Aurangabad) and Gujarat (Ahmedabad, Surat) dominate cotton textiles due to:
• Local cotton cultivation providing raw material advantage
• Historical development since British period
• Port connectivity for imports/exports
• Established industrial ecosystem
Statement 2: Jute industry faces decline because:
• Synthetic bags (polypropylene, plastic) are cheaper and durable
• Packaging industry shifting to alternatives
• Environmental awareness creating some revival, but overall trend is declining
Statement 3: India is indeed world's second-largest textile exporter (after China) with ~$40 billion annual exports, competing globally in cotton textiles, garments, and home textiles.

opt.t: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): The IT industry in India shows spatial concentration in metropolitan cities.
Reason (R): IT industry requires high-quality infrastructure, skilled manpower, and international connectivity.

so.t:
Assertion is CORRECT: IT industry concentration in metros is evident:
• Bangalore (28% of IT exports)
• Hyderabad, Chennai, Pune, Mumbai forming major IT hubs
• Tier-1 cities dominating software exports and employment
Reason is CORRECT and explains the assertion: IT industry requirements drive metropolitan concentration:
• Infrastructure: Reliable power, telecommunications, internet connectivity
• Skilled manpower: Engineering graduates, English proficiency, technical skills
• International connectivity: Airports for client visits, time zone advantages
• Support services: Banking, legal, logistics facilitating business operations

opt.t: Hooghly Industrial Belt - Jute and engineering goods

q.t: Which of the following industrial regions is correctly matched with its primary characteristic?

so.t:
All options are CORRECTLY matched:
(A) Mumbai-Pune belt:
• Heavy engineering: BHEL, L&T, Bajaj Auto, Tata Motors
• Chemicals: Reliance, ONGC, petrochemicals, pharmaceuticals
• Diversified industrial base with port advantages
(B) Hooghly Industrial Belt (Kolkata region):
• Jute industry: Traditional strength, though declining
• Engineering goods: Heavy electrical, railway equipment, steel-based industries
• Historical industrial development dating to British period
(C) Bangalore-Mysore corridor:
• Information Technology: Software capital with major IT parks
• Biotechnology: Biocon, research institutions, pharmaceutical R&D
• Knowledge-based industries leveraging educational institutions

q.t:
The concept of 'Industrial Clusters' in India aims to:
1. Achieve economies of scale through concentrated production
2. Reduce infrastructure costs through shared facilities
3. Enhance competitiveness in global markets
4. Promote balanced regional development
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
All objectives are CORRECT for India's Industrial Cluster Policy:
1. Economies of scale: Clustering similar industries reduces per-unit costs through bulk purchasing, shared resources, and specialized services.
2. Infrastructure cost reduction: Common effluent treatment plants, power substations, testing labs, and logistics facilities shared among cluster units.
3. Global competitiveness: Clusters create ecosystem for innovation, quality improvement, and export promotion (e.g., Tirupur textiles, Ludhiana woollens).
4. Regional development: Policy specifically targets backward regions and aims to create industrial growth poles in underdeveloped areas.
Examples of successful clusters:
• Tirupur (textiles) - global export hub
• Coimbatore (textiles/engineering) - diversified cluster
• Pune (automotive) - integrated supply chain
Policy framework: Cluster Development Programme under Ministry of MSME implements this strategy.

opt.t: Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I
Both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct, but Statement–II does not explain Statement–I
Statement–I is correct, but Statement–II is incorrect
Statement–I is incorrect, but Statement–II is correct

q.t:
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I:
The central bank holds gold in its reserves primarily to diversify its assets base.
Statement-II:
Gold acts as a hedge against inflation and foreign currency risks.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

so.t:
Recently, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has shifted over 100 tonnes of gold from the United Kingdom to its vaults in the country. This is the first time since 1991 that the precious metal has been added to the stock kept in the country.
● Central banks hold gold as part of their reserves to diversify their assets. Gold is considered a valuable asset that can provide stability and security, particularly in times of economic uncertainty or when foreign currency assets may be subject to fluctuations.
● Gold is widely recognized as a hedge against inflation and foreign currency risks. During periods of high inflation, the value of gold tends to rise. Additionally, gold can protect against foreign currency risks, as its value is not directly tied to any single currency and it tends to maintain its value over time.
So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II explains Statement–I.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
More than half of RBI’s gold reserves are held overseas in safe custody with the Bank of England and the Bank of International Settlements, and about a third of it is stored domestically. The move will also help RBI save storage costs, which are paid to the Bank of England. According to the annual data released by the RBI, the central held 822.10 tonnes worth of gold as a part of its foreign exchange reserves as of March 31, 2024, up from 794.63 tonnes held during the same period last year.

q.t:
Which of the following statements about India's Five-Year Plans is/are correct?
1. The First Five-Year Plan (1951-56) emphasized agricultural development.
2. The Second Five-Year Plan focused on heavy industries and was based on the Mahalanobis model.
3. The Planning Commission was replaced by NITI Aayog in 2015.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

so.t:
All statements are CORRECT:
Statement 1: First Five-Year Plan (1951-56) prioritized agriculture and irrigation:
• Focused on food grain production and irrigation projects
• Aimed at addressing food shortages and agricultural rehabilitation post-Partition
• Major projects: Bhakra Nangal, Damodar Valley Corporation
Statement 2: Second Plan (1956-61) emphasized heavy industries:
• Based on P.C. Mahalanobis model emphasizing capital goods
• Established steel plants at Rourkela, Bhilai, Durgapur
• Industrial growth rate target: 10% annually
Statement 3: Planning Commission dissolved and NITI Aayog established on January 1, 2015:
• Shift from centralized planning to cooperative federalism
• Focus on competitive and strategic planning

q.t:
The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) most relevant to India's current development challenges include:
1. SDG 1: No Poverty
2. SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation
3. SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy
4. SDG 11: Sustainable Cities and Communities
Which of the above SDGs align with India's national flagship programs?

so.t:
All SDGs align with India's flagship programs:
SDG 1 (No Poverty): Aligns with:
• PM-KISAN scheme for farmer income support
• Mahatma Gandhi NREGA for rural employment
• PM Jan Dhan Yojana for financial inclusion
SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation): Aligns with:
• Swachh Bharat Mission for sanitation
• Jal Jeevan Mission for piped water supply
• Namami Gange program for river cleaning
SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy): Aligns with:
• National Solar Mission (75 GW target)
• PM Ujjwala Yojana for clean cooking fuel
• Rural electrification programs
SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities): Aligns with:
• Smart Cities Mission (100 cities)
• AMRUT for urban infrastructure
• Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana for housing
India's performance: India ranks 99th in the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Index according to the 2025 Sustainable Development Report by the UN Sustainable Development solutions Network. This marks the first time India has entered the top 100 among 167 countries, with a score of approximately 67 out of 100, showing significant improvement from its previous rankings (109th in 2024 and 112th in 2023)

opt.t: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

q.t:
Assertion (A): The Green Revolution in India led to regional disparities in agricultural development.
Reason (R): High-Yielding Variety (HYV) seeds were more suitable for irrigated areas of Punjab, Haryana, and Western Uttar Pradesh.

so.t:
Assertion is CORRECT: Green Revolution created significant regional disparities:
• Punjab, Haryana achieved high productivity growth
• Eastern and Southern states lagged behind
• Income gaps between farming communities widened
• Regional imbalances in agricultural infrastructure development
Reason is CORRECT and explains the assertion: HYV seeds required:
• Assured irrigation facilities (more available in northwestern states)
• Fertilizers and pesticides (better supply chains in Green Revolution states)
• Mechanization support (easier in flat terrain of Punjab-Haryana)
• Extension services and credit facilities
Geographical factors: Northwestern states had better irrigation (canal systems), suitable topography, and progressive farming practices.

q.t:
Consider the following pairs of planning concepts and their focus areas:
1. Blue Economy - Marine resource utilization
2. Circular Economy - Waste minimization and resource recycling
3. Digital Economy - Technology-driven economic growth
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?

so.t:
All pairs are CORRECTLY matched:
Blue Economy: Sustainable use of ocean resources for economic growth:
• India's Blue Economy Policy focuses on fisheries, aquaculture, marine biotechnology
• Deep Ocean Mission launched for marine resource exploration
• Sagarmala program for port-led development
Circular Economy: Minimizing waste through reuse, recycling, and regeneration:
• India's Circular Economy approach in waste management
• Extended Producer Responsibility for packaging waste
• Plastic Waste Management Rules implementation
Digital Economy: Technology-driven economic transformation:
• Digital India mission promoting digital infrastructure
• JAM Trinity (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) enabling financial inclusion
